# Syntax changes (Part 1: db create & update)

## az sql db copy

### Before

```
Command
    az sql db copy: Create a copy of a database.

Arguments
    --dest-name [Required]: Name of the database that will be created as the copy destination.
    --dest-resource-group : Name of the resouce group to create the copy in. If unspecified,
                            defaults to the origin resource group.
    --dest-server         : Name of the server to create the copy in. If unspecified, defaults to
                            the origin server.
    --elastic-pool        : Name of the elastic pool to create the new database in.
    --no-wait             : Do not wait for the long-running operation to finish.
    --service-objective   : Name of the service objective for the new database.
    --tags                : Resource tags.

Resource Id Arguments
    --ids                 : One or more resource IDs (space-delimited). If provided, no other
                            'Resource Id' arguments should be specified.
    --name -n             : Name of the Azure SQL Database.
    --resource-group -g   : Name of resource group. You can configure the default group using `az
                            configure --defaults group=<name>`.
    --server -s           : Name of the Azure SQL server.
```

### After

```
Command
    az sql db copy: Create a copy of a database.
        A full list of performance level options can be seen by executing `az sql db list-editions
        -a -o table -l LOCATION`. The copy destination database must have the same edition as the
        source database, but you can change the edition after the copy has completed.

Arguments
    --dest-name [Required]: Name of the database that will be created as the copy destination.
    --dest-resource-group : Name of the resouce group to create the copy in. If unspecified,
                            defaults to the origin resource group.
    --dest-server         : Name of the server to create the copy in. If unspecified, defaults to
                            the origin server.
    --license-type        : The license type to apply for this database.  Allowed values: BasePrice,
                            LicenseIncluded.
    --no-wait             : Do not wait for the long-running operation to finish.
    --tags                : Resource tags.
    --zone-redundant -z   : Specifies whether to enable zone redundancy.  Allowed values: false,
                            true.

Performance Level (components) Arguments
    --capacity -c         : The capacity component of the sku in integer number of DTUs or vcores.
    --family -f           : The compute generation component of the sku (for vcore skus only).
                            Allowed values include: Gen4, Gen5.

Performance Level Arguments
    --elastic-pool        : The name or resource id of the elastic pool to create the database in.
    --service-objective   : The service objective for the new database. For example: Basic, S0, P1,
                            GP_Gen4_1, BC_Gen5_2.

Resource Id Arguments
    --ids                 : One or more resource IDs (space-delimited). If provided, no other
                            'Resource Id' arguments should be specified.
    --name -n             : Name of the Azure SQL Database.
    --resource-group -g   : Name of resource group. You can configure the default group using `az
                            configure --defaults group=<name>`.
    --server -s           : Name of the Azure SQL server.
```

## az sql db create

### Before

```
Command
    az sql db create: Create a database.

Arguments
    --name -n           [Required]: Name of the Azure SQL Database.
    --resource-group -g [Required]: Name of resource group. You can configure the default group
                                    using `az configure --defaults group=<name>`.
    --server -s         [Required]: Name of the Azure SQL server.
    --collation                   : The collation of the database. If createMode is not Default,
                                    this value is ignored.
    --edition                     : The edition of the database.
    --elastic-pool                : The name of the elastic pool the database is in. If
                                    elasticPoolName and requestedServiceObjectiveName are both
                                    updated, the value of requestedServiceObjectiveName is ignored.
                                    Not supported for DataWarehouse edition.
    --max-size                    : The max storage size of the database. Only the following sizes
                                    are supported (in addition to limitations being placed on each
                                    edition): 100MB, 500MB, 1GB, 5GB, 10GB, 20GB, 30GB, 150GB,
                                    200GB, 500GB. If no unit is specified, defaults to bytes (B).
    --no-wait                     : Do not wait for the long-running operation to finish.
    --sample-name                 : Indicates the name of the sample schema to apply when creating
                                    this database. If createMode is not Default, this value is
                                    ignored. Not supported for DataWarehouse edition.
    --service-objective           : The name of the configured service level objective of the
                                    database. This is the service level objective that is in the
                                    process of being applied to the database. Once successfully
                                    updated, it will match the value of serviceLevelObjective
                                    property. To see possible values, query the capabilities API (/s
                                    ubscriptions/{subscriptionId}/providers/Microsoft.Sql/locations/
                                    {locationID}/capabilities) referred to by operationId:
                                    "Capabilities_ListByLocation." or use the Azure CLI command `az
                                    sql db list-editions --location <location> --query
                                    [].supportedServiceLevelObjectives[].name`.
    --tags                        : Resource tags.
    --zone-redundant -z           : Specifies whether to enable zone redundancy for the database.
                                    Allowed values: false, true.
```

### After

```
Command
    az sql db create: Create a database.
        A full list of performance level options can be seen by executing `az sql db list-editions
        -a -o table -l LOCATION`.

Arguments
    --name -n           [Required]: Name of the Azure SQL Database.
    --resource-group -g [Required]: Name of resource group. You can configure the default group
                                    using `az configure --defaults group=<name>`.
    --server -s         [Required]: Name of the Azure SQL server.
    --license-type                : The license type to apply for this database.  Allowed values:
                                    BasePrice, LicenseIncluded.
    --max-size                    : The max storage size. If no unit is specified, defaults to bytes
                                    (B).
    --no-wait                     : Do not wait for the long-running operation to finish.
    --tags                        : Resource tags.
    --zone-redundant -z           : Specifies whether to enable zone redundancy.  Allowed values:
                                    false, true.

Creation Arguments
    --catalog-collation           : Collation of the metadata catalog.  Allowed values:
                                    DATABASE_DEFAULT, SQL_Latin1_General_CP1_CI_AS.
    --collation                   : The collation of the database.
    --sample-name                 : The name of the sample schema to apply when creating this
                                    database.

Performance Level (components) Arguments
    --capacity -c                 : The capacity component of the sku in integer number of DTUs or
                                    vcores.
    --edition --tier -e           : The edition component of the sku. Allowed values include: Basic,
                                    Standard, Premium, GeneralPurpose, BusinessCritical.
    --family -f                   : The compute generation component of the sku (for vcore skus
                                    only). Allowed values include: Gen4, Gen5.

Performance Level Arguments
    --elastic-pool                : The name or resource id of the elastic pool to create the
                                    database in.
    --service-objective           : The service objective for the new database. For example: Basic,
                                    S0, P1, GP_Gen4_1, BC_Gen5_2.
```

## az sql db restore

### Before

```
ommand
    az sql db restore: Create a new database by restoring from a backup.

Arguments
    --dest-name [Required]: Name of the database that will be created as the restore destination.
    --edition             : The edition for the new database.
    --elastic-pool        : Name of the elastic pool to create the new database in.
    --no-wait             : Do not wait for the long-running operation to finish.
    --service-objective   : Name of service objective for the new database.
    --tags                : Resource tags.

Resource Id Arguments
    --ids                 : One or more resource IDs (space-delimited). If provided, no other
                            'Resource Id' arguments should be specified.
    --name -n             : Name of the Azure SQL Database.
    --resource-group -g   : Name of resource group. You can configure the default group using `az
                            configure --defaults group=<name>`.
    --server -s           : Name of the Azure SQL server.

Restore Point Arguments
    --deleted-time        : If specified, restore from a deleted database instead of from an
                            existing database. Must match the deleted time of a deleted database in
                            the same server. Either --time or --deleted-time (or both) must be
                            specified.
    --time -t             : The point in time of the source database that will be restored to create
```

> Hypothesis-style randomized exploration of the space of scheduling decisions, with some clever heuristics to guide the search towards edge cases that are likely to find bugs, and maybe even hypothesis-style minimization.

This sounds like a case for writing a scheduler on top of Hypothesis!  Check out [generic state machines](https://hypothesis.readthedocs.io/en/latest/stateful.html#generic-state-machines) - if you can define a `steps()` method that returns (a `sampled_from` strategy of) a sequence of possible tasks to schedule, and an `execute_step(task)` method that schedules that task, Hypothesis will do the rest :smile: 

Of course, that means that most of the work has just moved one layer up...

- The fundamental part is the code to take an async function and output a `steps()` and `execute_step(step)` implementation.  At this point it should "just work", and though it could be more powerful I would expect to find some real bugs if you leave it running.
- Improve the `steps()` method from arbitrary order to an ordering based on useful heuristics.  This may involve writing a strategy that acts more like weighted decision tree than sampling from a list, and/or working out ways to observe more information we can use to bias the schedule.
- Consider fault injection, e.g. for anything that would usually hit the network.  Add arbitrary delays or back-pressure, make the network go down (and come up) unexpectedly, etc; may be related to #77.

And at this point the result could accurately be described as a state-of-the-art fuzzing scheduler :rocket:

Ah, right, that's another option I forgot to mention: using Trio to implement a new *Qt* event loop backend. This is possible in theory, by implementing the [`QAbstractEventDispatcher` interface](https://doc.qt.io/qt-5/qabstracteventdispatcher.html). Regarding pylint and `trio.testing`: I dug into this a little more.

It looks like the library that pylint actually uses to do static analysis is [astroid](https://astroid.readthedocs.io/en/latest/).

With astroid, I can get a list of all the names that it thinks are in the `trio` module like this:

```python
# Get a node representing accessing a variable named "trio"
ref_node = astroid.extract_node("import trio; trio")
# Ask astroid for the object that it thinks this variable refers to – the actual trio module
# .infer() is a generator, so we need to use list() to force its evaluation
module = list(ref_node.infer())[0]
# Get a list of the names astroid thinks are exported by this module
module.public_names()
```

This list does *not* include `testing`, with current trio.

However, we can also ask a slightly different question: "if someone mentions `trio.testing`, can you figure out what that means?"

```python
# This succeeds (and returns a module object for trio.testing)
ref_node = astroid.extract_node("import trio; trio.testing")
list(ref_node.infer())

# If we try the same trick on a bad name, we get an 'InferenceError' exception
ref_node = astroid.extract_node("import trio; trio.testingxxx")
list(ref_node.infer())
```

So it looks like astroid's two ways of accessing this information don't quite agree. I guess this makes sense – pylint is primarily designed to try to make sense of existing code that contains references like `trio.testing`, and mapping those back to their origin; starting from a module and generating an accurate completion list for it isn't really something it ever needs to do.

This does suggest an interesting thing we could do though: for each name exported by the `trio` namespace, we could ask whether astroid can figure out what it refers to, by doing something like:

```python
for name in trio.__dict__:
    if name.startswith("_"):
        continue
    source = "import trio; trio.{}".format(name)
    # I'm not sure why sometimes this raises an error and other times it return Uninferable
    found = list(astroid.extract_node(source).infer())
    assert len(found) > 0
    assert found[0] is not astroid.Uninferable
```

This is a much stronger test than just "can you figure out that this name is defined?". It actually requires that astroid can track its import chain all the back to the original object. So we might not want to do this now, because we probably can't pass this test until we finish fixing `trio._core`. But eventually this is probably a useful test, because this stronger kind of analysis is what you need to do things like type checking.

Yes, I can do everything before the calling, but the caller(intermediate app) may not be equipped with a `db`, but I now think out of a simpler solution. Just put the setup of a nested app inside the startup of the caller(based on your idea, or maybe I should read more docs :( ). 
In the original code I put those process inside the nested apps, so came the problem. 
Here's my example, and they work well
```python
from aiohttp import web


# the inner app
app_a = web.Application()


async def index_of_a(request):
    return web.Response(text=request.app['db'])


app_a.router.add_get('/', index_of_a)


# the setup process of app a should be controlled inside 
# the startup of app b
async def setup_db_of_inner_app(app):
    app_a['db'] = app['db']


app_b = web.Application()
# a as the nested app of b
app_b.on_startup.append(setup_db_of_inner_app)
app_b.add_subapp('/a', app_a)

# the outer app
app_c = web.Application()


# take care of the setup of app b
async def db_of_app_a_and_setup_db_of_intermediate_app(app):
    # first setup the outer app
    app['db'] = 'the db'
    # then the intermediate, namely the `direct child` of outer app
    app_b['db'] = app['db']


app_c.on_startup.append(db_of_app_a_and_setup_db_of_intermediate_app)
app_c.add_subapp('/b', app_b)
web.run_app(app_c)
```
That's what I mean by `insert a startup before the startup of the sub app`.

Because Django's view almost never starts new thread. 
But people calls `loop.create_task()` from handlers very often *right now* without support from aiohttp.
And they handle these tasks wrong in 95% cases as I mentioned in very first message.
That's why we need provide a reliable tool to help them write error-safe code.

If your code performs CPU bound tasks like `numpy` computations -- you must run this code inside an executor.

`aiojobs` is for spawning asynchronous code, it doesn't prevent running blocking sync one or execute a task inside an executor.

I recommend switch on [asyncio debug mode](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-dev.html#asyncio-debug-mode) to catch all problematic places.

The worker is running in main thread, if it is blocked -- you are in problem.
I don't want using signal handlers directly, they are moved to event loop for a good reason.

Interesting findings. This tp_profile script is testing a very nonrealistic special case, but you're right that the old TP is faster in this case.

This script:

- Uses random inputs
- Uses ~50% sparsity

So if there are 2048 columns, ~1024 of them are active. So, 1024 different segments have to grow / reinforce synapses every timestep. The inputs are random, so none of the synapses are useful. It's hard to predict the exact outcome, since the 50% sparsity might cause tons of false positives, so segments might actually activate quite often despite the random input.

One reason the old TP is faster in this case is because its synapse cleanup code is better. This experiment stress-tests the heck out of synapse cleanup. The flaw with the TM's code (it's actually buggy, not just slow) is that its synapse cleanup deletes one synapse at a time, rather than doing them in groups like the TP's `freeNSynapses` method. This is slower, and doing it one at a time is wrong because if you create 10 synapses and it triggers one-at-a-time cleanup, you might effectively only add 1 synapse, repeatedly cleaning up the just-added synapse. This is a known issue. I'm planning on making segment/synapse cleanup better, you can see [I've been studying for it](http://mrcslws.com/blocks/2016/05/05/tp-segment-cleanup.html).

One reason the TM/TP become slower than the SP in this case is that the SP is more capable of handling non-sparse inputs. Since it uses connection matrices rather than lists of synapses, the change in sparsity doesn't have a dramatic effect on the overlap calculation.

Usually when we talk about the performance of the algorithms, we use non-random inputs. And since you're feeding patterns directly into the TM, you need to think about sparsity.

On Feb 20, 2017 12:32 AM, "Cosimo Lupo" <notifications@github.com> wrote:

I'm curious where cff was indicated

the OP typed fontmake -u NotoSansLao-Bold.ufo, so fontmake by default tries
to compile both OTF and TTF, in that order.

OK cool :)

However, the AssertionError in the traceback above was *not* caused by
fontmake trying to create a CFF-OTF from a UFO containing TrueType outlines
(this is not supported yet, depends on googlei18n/cu2qu#29
<https://github.com/googlei18n/cu2qu/pull/29> being landed first).

Aha :)

The problem is that booleanOperations can't remove overlaps from contours
with TrueType outlines ("qcurve"), and fontmake by default attempts to
remove the overlaps, unless told otherwise with --keep-overlaps option.

It seems to me that removing overlaps by default in TTF is suboptimal. Why
is this done?

I haven't checked the source files, but if they contain quadratic contours,
it's likely that these were generated by decompiling binary TTF rather than
drawn that way, and hence they may already have overlaps removed. Passing
options --keep-overlaps -o ttf should complete successfully

> It still could be an issue for the field state leaking across multiple dump() calls with different arguments on the same schema instance.

Since `dump()` doesn't accept an `only` parameter, and field instances aren't shared between schema instances, there is no new field state that is created between `dump()` calls, so it appears that `only_explicit` is unnecessary, an `only` could be applied to the nested field directly.

It is a little misleading that `_update_fields()` is called when the schema is initialized (without a data object), then again in every `dump()` (with the data object it is serializing). Nearly everything that `_update_fields()` does during initialization is repeated unnecessarily during a `dump()` (mainly `__set_field_attrs()`).

That said I think this feature can safely be implemented without modifying `fields.py`. Since we should think of modifications to field instances in `_update_fields()` as initialization logic (which are never changed during a dump), so modifying `only` on a `Nested` field during `_update_fields()` semantically is the same as initializing it with the nested `only` fields supplied to the parent field.

```SQL
CREATE TABLE Entity(
```

It's intentional, yes, because this is always what you want for most common types of models -- if you have a categorical variable in your formula, then patsy interprets that to mean that you want the design matrix to allow for the different levels of that category to have different values. In a regular linear regression model, the design matrix you describe would encode the assumption that items that fall into category `A` should always be predicted to have an exactly zero response, while response for items in category `B` will be estimated from the data, which is a bit odd :-).

The canonical documentation on how patsy interprets formulas and why is [here](https://patsy.readthedocs.org/en/latest/formulas.html) (though you don't have read this, it's just FYI).

Can you elaborate on what you're trying to do and why?

(There's a comment on #60 mentioning one possible use case for things like this -- Cox proportional hazards models where the intercept is unidentifiable, but I think the right solution for the Cox case is something involving the model itself telling patsy to ensure that the design matrix's output span should always have the intercept removed; I don't know if that would be relevant to your case or not.)

@mrshu The reason why I use trailing whitespaces to indicate that paragraph continues is that `formatoptions+=a` (i.e. automatic paragraph reformatting) needs to be fought with in any other case. This reformatting is rather convenient with trailing whitespace, without it is a nightmare that tries to shift indent when it is not needed or join separate paragraphs. With whitespace to stop this one just needs to remove the whitespace.

As you saw `patch_cli` decorator before almost every test case in `test_mirror_view.py`, it will patch the `cli` and instead sending real requests to VNX it reads the corresponding mocked `naviseccli` command output from `storops_test/vnx/testdata/block_output`.

What you need to do is:
1. Prepare your test case codes. Decorate with `patch_cli`.
2. Be clear about what exactly naviseccli command would it be when you invoke like `VNXMirrorView.create(t_cli(), 'mv0', 245)`.
3. After you get the `naviseccli` command of step 2 is: `naviseccli ... mirror -sync -create -name mv0 -lun 245 -usewriteintentlog -o`, then you can add a file `mirror_-sync_-create_-name_mv0_-lun_245_-usewriteintentlog_-o.txt` to `block_output` folder.
4. Your test is ready to go.

Some tips:
1. You could use pycharm to make it quick to finish your tests.
1.1 Run `tox -e py27` under `storops` folder. You'll get a virtualenv under `.tox/py27`
1.2 Open Pycharm settings to set the Python interpreter to the python under 1.1's virtualenv.
1.3 In Pycharm, navigate file test_mirror_view.py, then you could `debug Unittest run` any test case there. And you could check the output of test running to see which mock data is read, or set break points there to check detail back trace.

2. If you're using command line, just use `tox -e py27 -- -k VNXMirrorViewTest` to run all the cases of mirror view. Or specify detailed case name to `-k` parameter: `tox -e py27 -- -k VNXMirrorViewTest.test_create_success`

I finally found the problem:

On https://github.com/plone/plone.app.portlets/blob/master/plone/app/portlets/dashboard.py#L58 ``plone.dashboard3`` is assigned to an empty list rather than an assignment.

plone.protect whitelists portlet assignments specifically, so they can really write on read, but an empty list on ``plone.dashboard3`` is an ``BTree``, so the traceback is printed.

One possible solution would be to assign a random portlet assignment (I tested it locally with the rss one and it works, I expect any other to work as well).

Thanks, that's a very ambitious PR!

I'm afraid it might be a bit too much for the scope of the country template, and makes assumptions that we can not be certain of.

For example, shipping a list of all existing countries is a strong limitation: some of the “country packages” are not necessarily _countries_ in the list of nation states: the township of Barcelona has modelled [their tax and benefit system](https://github.com/jvalduvieco/openfisca-barcelona) from this country template, and would not have been able to start with this user interface.

In a similar fashion, the New Zealand instance is named “[Aotearoa](https://github.com/ServiceInnovationLab/openfisca-aotearoa)”, after the Maōri name of the country. It is impossible to define these elements beforehand, and forcing reusers to go through this limited list is a very constraining.

Adding a graphical user interface is also quite a burden for maintenance. We would need to test this in all supported environments (Windows, macOS, GNU/Linux), and I am not confident we have the resources for such an investment.

Thank you again for your contribution. I will unfortunately have to go forward and close this PR.
If someone else from @openfisca/core-contributors wants to have another look and maybe help you go forward with automated GUI tests and adding free-form fields, they can reopen this as such a feature would definitely add a more professional feeling to OpenFisca!
In its current state, however, it is unfortunately a bit beyond what I believe we can maintain 🙂

While there is [code to handle stack alignment](https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/master/angr/calling_conventions.py#L553) this code is never executed.

```python
# handle alignment
while claripy.is_true((state.regs.sp + self.STACKARG_SP_DIFF) % self.STACK_ALIGNMENT != 0):
    state.regs.sp -= 1
```

 It appears the reason for this is that the expression `(state.regs.sp + self.STACKARG_SP_DIFF) % self.STACK_ALIGNMENT != 0` returns a `SimActionObject`, whereas `claripy.is_true` expects an `ast` and thus always returns `False`.

While replacing `claripy.is_true` with `state.solver.is_true` I guess and even better option would be to replace the while loop entirely with

```python
# handle alignment
alignment = (state.regs.sp + self.STACKARG_SP_DIFF) % self.STACK_ALIGNMENT
state.regs.sp -= alignment
```

> How do people know that if they click on "examples" that they would get some help directly printed there?

They don't have to know they just hope that it's there 😆 

> It's true that it's "one click away" when browsing GitHub, but is it really such a big deal?

From UX point of view - yes. For a Python developer who never had to see a Makefile in his/her life because she never wrote a single line of code in language that needs to be compiled.

Some additional notes and precisions:

> For new contributors it's not easy to know which to use when

This is not a problem in my eyes due to three reasons.  Firstly, our QA signatures are [well documented](http://invenio-software.org/wiki/Tools/Git/Workflow#R2.Remarksoncommitlogmessages).  Secondly, we are using standard signatures known e.g. from the Linux world.  Thirdly, the development tools usually offer all these signatures out of the box, e.g. Emacs's [git-modes](https://github.com/magit/git-modes) provides:

- C-c C-s inserts Signed-off-by (`git-commit-signoff')
- C-c C-a inserts Acked-by (`git-commit-ack')
- C-c C-t inserts Tested-by (`git-commit-test')
- C-c C-r inserts Reviewed-by (`git-commit-review')
- C-c C-o inserts Cc (`git-commit-cc')
- C-c C-p inserts Reported-by (`git-commit-reported')

So even if new developers don't know these "standard" signature conventions upon their arrival, they can learn it quickly from the documentation, it can be a useful knowledge for them for other personal git based projects, and putting proper signature is very cheap operation, just a keystroke away.

> additionally it's not clear if the differences in signatures is actually used.

This is the core of the RFC to me.  We now have basically three QA signatures coming from reviewers, each denoting different confidence level, in the increasing order of quality:

- Acked-by
- Tested-by
- Reviewed-by

Considering the high turnover of developers, these might be useful, for example imagine someone bisecting some code written by somebody else N years ago;  the person may want to know whether the code was reviewed only lightly or really carefully.

So the core question of this RFC is: do we make use of this confidence level distinction in real life or not?  If we do, it it worth keeping, because it is both cheap and standard.  If we don't, it is worth simplifying to only `Signed-off-by`.  Note that this could mean going for the highest QA standard that this signature implies, because it won't be possible to tell anymore a tester from a reviewer.

> Tester/reviewers should only sign commits they can fully confirm the quality off.

With one kind of sign-off signature only, it will be impossible to tell whether John acted as a tester here or as a full reviewer when he signs.  OT1H, this difference could be important in hunting bugs N years from now, as mentioned above.   OTOH, if we don't make use of it, and if don't have an armada of separate testers in the team, and if people *always* tend to deeply review rather than only test, then this distinction is not so important.

Here are stats about the current usage:

```console
$ git log master | grep -c Acked-by:
30
$ git log master | grep -c Tested-by:
259
$ git log master | grep -c Reviewed-by:
651
```

Prevent content from being framed and clickjacked

- ``1``: Enables the filter
- ``mode=block``: prevents rendering the page

**X-Content-Type-Options** ``nosniff``
Will prevent the browser from MIME-sniffing a response away from the declared content-type

- ``nosniff``: prevent the browser from MIME-sniffing a response away from the declared content-type.


**Content-Security-Policy (CSP)** ``default-src 'self'``
Restrict rescources to prevent XSS/other attacks (i.e. inline scripts)

- ``default-src 'self'``: which scripts the protected resource can execute

**X-Download-Options** ``noopen``
Prevent downloads from opening

- ``noopen``: prevents files from opening

**Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS)** ``max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains``
Prevent downgrade attacks (https to http)

- ``max-age=31536000``: The time, in seconds, that the browser should remember that this site is only to be accessed using HTTPS
- ``includeSubDomains``: If this optional parameter is specified, this rule applies to all of the site's subdomains as well.

Restrict content loaded by flash

@jirikuncar @lnielsen this is still WIP but I would like to know what you think of this.

The endpoint configuration is parsed by a Marshmallow schema which creates a config object. This config handles endpoint vs default values, thus the defaults can be overriden in deposit by replacing the schema class.

The schema is used by the extension's `init_app` and the result is given to the `create_blueprint` function. This enables to change the configuration schema and result classes and still use `create_blueprint`.

The config is given to each View class. It is then added to the application context on each REST request thanks to the decorator `with_endpoint_config`. The endpoint_config can be retrieved from anywhere by using `config_loader.current_endpoint_config`.

There is one thing I don't know, it is if we use the endpoint_config for everything. For example I started removing the `links_factory` from the serializers arguments. The base serializer can directly retrieve the `links_factory` by accessing `config_loader.current_endpoint_config`. Is this something we want?

Crate has a hard coded [internal select limit of 10000](https://github.com/crate/crate/blob/master/core/src/main/java/io/crate/Constants.java#L40) because of two reasons:
* prevent performance issues, e.g. when unintendedly fetching without LIMIT
* Crate does not support paging to the clients yet, which could lead to out of memory exceptions when selecting a large amount of data.

The latter is the more important reason.

---

Hi all,

I am happy to announce the release of version 0.2 of the regions package.

The regions package features classes to represent pixel and sky regions with methods to do analysis (mainly containment check and mask generation) and matplotlib plotting, as well as DS9 region format serialisation.

Docs: http://astropy-regions.readthedocs.io
Code: https://github.com/astropy/regions

As usual, you can upgrade with pip or conda:

pip install  regions —upgrade
conda upgrade -c astropy regions

This regions package is in an early stage of development. It is not feature complete or API stable. Feedback or contributions highly welcome!
There is a tutorial introduction available that gives an overview of the current features and API here:
http://astropy-regions.readthedocs.io/en/latest/getting_started.html

The 0.2 release contains significant additions such as a PixCoord, BoundingBox and Mask class, as well as efficient circle and polygon pixel containment and mask code written in Cython, added by Tom Robitaille, Larry Bradley and Christoph Deil. Adam Ginsburg implemented several improvements and fixes to the DS9 region serialisation compared to v0.1.

The hope is that regions package development accelerates a bit and that we will reach a status where it’s appropriate to propose it for inclusion in the Astropy core as astropy.regions in time for the Astropy 2.0 release (i.e. by March or April 2017 latest).

Questions, feedback and contributions on this mailing list or on Github are very welcome!

Cheers,
Christoph

pyarrow 0.9.0 contains a packaging problem (iirc), and the wheels they built for macOS couldn’t be installed. To remedy this (you cannot re-upload a file with the same name on PyPI, in case you’re wondering), they released 0.9.0.post1, which *only contains macOS wheels*. This means that when you `pip install pyarrow`, you get 0.9.0.post1 on macOS, and 0.9.0 everywhere else.

When Pipenv does locking, it needs to (well, tries to) make the lock file cross-platform. With a wildcard installation (`pyarrow = "*"`), it needs to find a latest version for all platforms—but there isn’t one! pyarrow 0.9.0.post1 only works on macOS, and 0.9.0 does not work on macOS (they deleted the broken macOS wheels). That’s why locking fails.

pip works because it only needs to care about the platform you’re running on. There is a valid latest version no matter where it is running, and that’s good enough for pip.

Knowing the root cause, you can actually work around this problem by `pipenv install "pyarrow!=0.9.0,!=0.9.0.post1"`. As long as Pipenv does not need to consider those versions, it can resolve pyarrow correctly (to 0.8.0, until a newer version is released).

I think the flat structure makes more sense. The lock file is supposed to be able to reflect an exact snapshot of the virtualenv, so it can deterministically reproduce it on another machine. Since Python always flattens dependencies (unlike, say, Node), the lock file should naturally also be flat. It doesn’t have to be, theoretically, but that would complicate things unnecessarily IMO.

There is nothing wrong with keeping two graphs. It’s just both graphs need to be flat, and self-contained. So the two graphs should be regular, and regular + dev (instead of regular and dev). This way `pipenv install --dev` can work solely on the latter graph, providing a consistent behaviour.

Rational:
* using `tox` should work as before (same as `tox -e python`, by adding `envlist=python` to `tox.ini`)
* coverage is not enabled by default (it's slow)
* disable coverage for pypy and pypy3 (way too slow)
* add coverage info for `pkg_resources`, and make sure tests are ignored (fix `coverage:run` section in `tox.ini`)
* make sure all CI jobs are identifiable (using `CODECOV_ENV`/`APPVEYOR_JOB_NAME`/`TRAVIS_JOB_NAME`)
* again, I don't see the point in treating xfailed tests differently, especially when this results in executing the tests suite 2 times: now I have to parse 2 different reports, and what about the interaction with pytest options (like `--last-failed`, or `--last-failed-no-failures`)?
* disable report on terminal: for the CI jobs, that's what codecov is for, and locally, it's much more user-friendly to call `tox -e coverage -- html` and look at the output for example

Thanks for the report, but this is a misunderstanding of how the eggs/sdists search is supposed to work. It's not supposed to look for these eggs along sys.path of _either_ interpreter, it's supposed to look for them relative to the current directory and to the `virtualenv.py` file that is being executed. This allows the right eggs/sdists to be bundled along with `virtualenv.py` (as they are if you `pip install virtualenv`). In other words, it was never the intent of this code to find an _installed_ egg, it was always the intent to find an egg that was intentionally bundled with virtualenv.py for this purpose. Specifically, the locations searched are:

1. The current directory.
2. The directory where virtualenv.py is located.
3. A `virtualenv_support` directory relative to the directory where virtualenv.py is located.
4. If the file being executed is not named virtualenv.py (i.e. is a boot script), a `virtualenv_support` directory relative to wherever `virtualenv.py` is actually installed.

You mention that `grad` only applies to scalar valued functions. What if I define a scalar valued function like this:

```py
import autograd.numpy as np

def logdet(X):
    return np.sum(np.log(np.linalg.eigh(X)[0]))
```

The gradient of `np.divide` is [defined in numpy_grads.py](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/ca7ee9e27c5df3e5b2a39969c9adff7fe6ca32f8/autograd/numpy/numpy_grads.py#L73-L74):

```python
anp.divide.defgrad(lambda ans, x, y : unbroadcast(ans, x, lambda g :   g / y))
anp.divide.defgrad(lambda ans, x, y : unbroadcast(ans, y, lambda g : - g * x / y**2), argnum=1)
```

The anonymous function in the first line essentially implements the multiplication rule for ∂/∂x x/y (for computing the derivative with respect to the first argument of `divide`) and the anonymous function in the second line essentially implements the quotient rule for ∂/∂y x/y (for computing the derivative with respect to the second argument of `divide`, both in reverse mode. The [`unbroadcast` function](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/ca7ee9e27c5df3e5b2a39969c9adff7fe6ca32f8/autograd/numpy/numpy_grads.py#L392-L410) effectively undoes the broadcasting that may happen to the arguments of `np.divide`.

The `defgrad` method just attaches those anonymous functions to the wrapped `divide` object so that autograd can find them when it needs to compute the reverse-mode gradient of `divide`.

That's it for the autograd implementation. If your class is meant to implement its own reverse-mode functions and not use autograd, you probably just need the gradient definitions in those anonymous functions. 

Hope that helps you write your code!

You're not pushing your luck on number 5. It mostly depends on what kind of "equality" you want. You can do direct equality between two notebooks made with commutable using [Immutable's `isEqual`](http://facebook.github.io/immutable-js/docs/#/Map/equals):

```
notebook.isEqual(otherNotebook)
```

or

```
Immutable.is(notebook, otherNotebook)
```

This is a fast O(1) computation because it's checking the hashCode between the two objects. It has some caveats to it though:

* The notebook itself needs to be a fully immutable object for this to be accurate
* This is useful for in-memory comparisons (has the notebook changed), not for comparing two notebooks on disk

Generally speaking, `commutable` is a utility wrapper around `immutable` setting up an opinionated data structure. What we probably need to do is link out to `Immutable` docs more. With that, I'll answer a few of the others:

```
function getCellById(notebook, id) {
  return notebook.getIn(['cellMap', id])
}
```

```
function getCellByIndex(notebook, index) {
  const id = notebook.getIn(['cellOrder', index]);
  return notebook.getIn(['cellMap', id])
}
```

Those would probably be fine to add to commutable itself. They're definitely useful for testing, though I imagine I probably wouldn't use them in applications built on top of commutable because I tend to use `updateIn` for performance when I have multiple operations on the document.

> I'm sure commutable isn't meant to be tied to nteract.

That's true. Originally it got built well before the desktop app, it was the start of opinions for what the in-memory data structure would be. I've been thinking on how I would use just the cells from commutable for things like https://github.com/nteract/nteract/pull/1986 or even dashboard / grid layouts.

F200 camera is passing hardware time of capture using first couple of pixels of the image. (this rather odd method is used in a lack of better alternative before UVC frame metadata) You can usually see these pixels flickering with a naked eye. Try connecting to a loom file:

```python
with loompy.connect("/Users/sten/build_20171205/L5_All.agg.loom") as ds:
```
```
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
KeyError                                  Traceback (most recent call last)
~/Code/loompy/loompy/layer_manager.py in __getattr__(self, name)
     85                 try:
---> 86                         vals = self.__dict__["storage"][name]
     87                         if vals is None:

KeyError: 'storage'
```

The reason is that you're now preventing setting regular class attributes on read-only files. The code in `__setattr__` is very non-trivial, because to support accessing layers as `ds.layers.exons`, the LayerManager intercepts member access (by implementing `__getattr__`). Yet, it still needs to have some place to store its own attributes (such as `LayerManager.ds` and `LayerManager.storage`). To accomplish this [without creating an infinite loop](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/17020115/how-to-use-setattr-correctly-avoiding-infinite-recursion), `__setattr__` looks for attributes starting with an exclamation mark, such as `!storage` and stores those as object attributes in `self.__dict__`. 

But this code:

```python
		if self._write_access:
```

```python
		storage: Dict[str, np.ndarray] = {}
		setattr(self, "!storage", storage)

		self._write_access = False
```

which explains the other exception I get, from inside `__setattr__`:

@tkelman - sorry for the confusion about the SHA.  I was trying to follow some of the debugging requests in [ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues-#8087](https://github.com/ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues/issues/8087#issuecomment-357402997).

My sequence of steps is:
1. Used Anaconda Windows installer to install a full python 3 environment in the last year.
2. Have updated the Anaconda environment using conda CLI, because updates through Anaconda navigator would fail trying to update Anaconda Navigotor.  But I believe I had an up-to-date Anaconda3/Python3/conda environment before the next step.
3. I've tried to add a Python2.7 environment following the steps in the conda docs "managing python".  ` conda create -n py27 python=2.7 anaconda`.  This failed on the ipykernel post link step mentioned here and in [ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues-#8087](https://github.com/ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues/issues/8087#issuecomment-357402997)
4. After posting this issue and finding the other issue, I've installed Python 2.7 `conda create -n py27 python=2.7 python` and a number of the other dependencies without ipykernel/jupyter, so I could drill down on the problem which appears to be `conda install -n py27 python=2.7 ipykernel`.

I've modified `./ipykernel-4.8.2-py27_0/Scripts/.ipykernel-post-link.bat` because it was sending all output to > NUL so the error message was being lost.   I believe this is the source of the SHA mismathch. That .bat now contains:

```
@echo on
echo test from .bat
:: Install kernelspec at post-link because conda doesn't substitute Windows paths correctly in JSON files
"%PREFIX%\python.exe" -m ipykernel install --sys-prefix  && if errorlevel 1 exit 1
```

Above I gave a couple one-liner commands for unix to fix up a 4.3.28 environment.  Easiest way I know of on Windows is to use a python script.  Open notepad, and copy the contents below to `c:\fix_timestamps.py`

```python
CONDA_ENVIRONMENT_PREFIX="c:\\ProgramData\\Anaconda3"  # <-- fill this in yourself
                                                       # backslashes must be doubled

from glob import glob
import json
import os
for path in glob(os.path.join(CONDA_ENVIRONMENT_PREFIX, 'conda-meta', '*.json')):
    with open(path) as fh:
        content = json.load(fh)
    if 'timestamp' in content:
        old_timestamp = content['timestamp']
        content['timestamp'] = int(old_timestamp)
        if old_timestamp != content['timestamp']:
            with open(path, 'w') as fh:
               fh.write(json.dumps(content, indent=2, sort_keys=True, separators=(',', ': ')))
```

Also, change the path in the variables `CONDA_ENVIRONMENT_PREFIX` to point to the conda environment you want to fix.  Then run the script with `python fix_timestamps.py`.

@damianavila when it says "Fetching package metadata..." it is _not_ necessarily going online. That means just what it says---it is trying to fetch the metadata. If that metadata is in the cache, it needn't go online, but it still has to get it from disk. Do you have evidence that it actually tried to connect to the Internet the second time around?

If the package metadata is not in the cache, or the packages themselves are not in the cache, then it has no choice but to go online. 

Don't get me wrong, there could still be some implementation issues here, but it _cannot clone_ without metadata.

From my latest [pull request](https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/2000). The one thing I'd like to see is some feedback in the second, `--no-update-deps` case. If someone knows there is a newer version of `pandas`, it would be important to tell them why we didn't find it. But this isn't quite as easy as it might first appear---probably requiring the full hint machinery.

```
Using Anaconda Cloud api site https://api.anaconda.org

pandas                    0.16.2               np19py34_0  
```

As a more general issue, perhaps conda's plan output ought to separate build strings and build numbers. 

On one hand, it might also be good for conda to consider that when a build string changes, that it needn't compare the build numbers. The problem is that there really is no current standard for combining the build number and the build string. In theory, the build string can be _anything_, and it need not include the build number at all. In _practice_, the build string is either the build number itself or something like `'%s_%d' % (build_prefix, build_number)`. We could perhaps detect if that's the case and parse appropriately. That is: if the build prefix changes, the build number is not considered when deciding if it's an upgrade/downgrade.

So scaling the weights at a single time step (or let's say initialization) would be equivalent in terms of ouptut, but you need to keep that constant in place. Let me try to describe the problem without a blackboard. So if we have 2 layer network with `h=tanh(W_1 x+b)' and the second layer is `h_2=tanh(W_2 h + b)`. No if I rescale `W_2` by `C` this is equivalent to rescaling the output of `h` by `C`. This is true. But however, this means that the landscapes in the parameter space of W_2 and W_1 are going to be very different (e.g. if C=2 in general the values of W_2 would need to be in the twice larger values of W_1). This is bad for 2 reasons. First, any L2 or L1 loss in general discards this information. Unless you want to weight each layer separately, but than this gets even more complicates as you have more hyperparameters. Secondly, and which is the reason I use it for, is because the objective function space get's better conditioned. Here I'm sure you've seen the pictures with the convex circle bowl  the one which is squished in one direction. Essentially, having to scale up `W_2` to get the same output values means that the objective will have this kind bad conditioning where on each subsequent layer your parameters would have to be larger in order to get good outputs. Thus, rescaling the non linearity instead by a constant tries to address this problem. If you want to rescale parameters you should have something like `W_2 = W_2hat * C` to use where C is fixed and your true parameters to be `W_2hat`. Than they will be equivalent.

@macisamuele - I'm sure it would work if I could specify the schema, but the application requires me to accept a variety of different object schemas, and since Swagger2.0 doesn't support anyOf, there isn't much I can do beyond using object.  Even if anyOf was supported there are scenarios where I simply don't know what the object will be until I receive it, so it's impossible to specify all of the possible schemas.

For example, in one case I need to supply JSON object inputs to a workflow engine, which vary depending on the workflow that is being executed, and the workflows can change over time.   I don't want to have to specify the schema for every possible workflow input object, which is impossible to keep up with, so I have to call them objects and rely on the workflow engine to do the validation.

Even in Python 2.7, `noload` is broken for ZODB. The code here uses its Python 2.6 behaviour (see #9), but that has [Python bug 1101399](https://bugs.python.org/issue1101399). That bug doesn't matter for the ZODB use case, but it makes `noload` less generic, which is a concern for the stdlib. 

Given that Python 2.7 has a `noload`, but it's broken for ZODB (and [the commit that broke it](https://github.com/python/cpython/commit/973f8b4ca6b2614968d76760c38f5adc31da2727#diff-3be8b5d321e9c11ae97240f8faea8db5) refers to it as an "undocumented, obscure function" and didn't bother to add a test case), and Python 3.x has *never* had a noload, I would imagine the barrier to get it added to Python 3 would be fairly high. It would have to fix the same bug that 2.7 did but without breaking in the more general case (as well as be documented and tested, presumably).

The window for feature additions to Python 3.7 has closed, so at the earliest such an addition would be part of is 3.8 and we would need this library as long as ZODB supported any versions < 3.8.

I'm not saying it can't happen, but it sounds like a lot of work 😄 I would guess that a starting point would be posting to python-ideas.

[zope.testrunner's documentation](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.testrunner/blob/673f70cdcf04a5333b1adf44c8922ae32c850f8f/src/zope/testrunner/tests/testrunner-layers-api.txt#L9) says "Layers are *generally* implemented as classes using class methods" (emphasis mine), which can be read to imply that they don't have to be.

Martin Aspeli describes some downsides of using classes themselves as test layers in this [email from 2010](https://mail.zope.org/pipermail/zope-dev/2010-April/040267.html).

Some market research:

- [ZODB](https://github.com/zopefoundation/ZODB/blob/adf92861902125c7ca70854d9354557806d45217/src/ZODB/tests/util.py#L99-L118) uses an object instance as the layer.
- [zope.app.testing.functional](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.app.testing/blob/master/src/zope/app/testing/functional.py#L320-L359), uses an object instance as the layer.
- [zope.app.wsgi](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.app.wsgi/blob/0a50583193daf85d16d81731a9b1358d8074e3f4/src/zope/app/wsgi/tests.py#L74-L94) uses object instances as the layer.
- [plone.testing](https://github.com/plone/plone.testing/blob/master/src/plone/testing/layer.py) defines object instances to be used as test layers.
- [gocept.selenium](https://bitbucket.org/gocept/gocept.selenium/src/aaa8166f5244c3fcc4506d17ffbe6166bafac5cf/src/gocept/selenium/webdriver.py?at=default#cl-33) builds on top of plone.testing and therefore also uses object instances as test layers.

In short, I don't think zope.testrunner can afford to drop support of object instances as test layers (or require that everyone go implement a `__mro__` property on their test layers) at this time, without a painful transition affecting many users.

I suggest that reimplementing the MRO algorithm inside zope.testrunner to compute the MRO from `layer.__bases__` is the right thing to do.  I volunteer to do it if nobody else steps forward. ;)

Hi @btashton, thanks for bringing up this issue. In release 1.0.6 I've added a 30 second timeout to post requests and I've wrapped it in a try catch to print the errors that might occur during transit. If the buffer is full and there are persistent network connection issues, the module will attempt to keep on appending to the buffer and then immediately flush. The byte limit set in the module is pretty far from the max bytes a request can post, so you should see several error messages before any logs are dropped. I think I like this proposal.  So to rephrase it in my own words (in a way that might be a start for some documentation, hence the present tense rather than conditional):

The galsim namespace and galsim python module has all the core functionality of the GalSim project.  We expect that pretty much everything someone would want to use when writing code that uses GalSim is accessible with just `import galsim` or `galsim::blah`.  

In addition, we have some sub-packages that we use within the GalSim project, that are in some sense separate, and we'd like to keep them that way.  They are within the galsim namespace/module, but they require further specification to get access to them.

integ has Mike Jarvis's integration code.
hsm has the Hirata, Seljak, and Mandelbaum moment fitting code.
(There will probably be more.)

To access integ (for example) in python, you should write `import galsim.integ`.  Within a C++ program, you would use `galsim::integ::` as the namespace.

This is a pretty complex issue unfortunately, and something lots of projects have trouble with. Here's an example of why.

We have a python package:
```
package
 | - __init__.py
 | - module1.py
 \ - module2.py
```

Say we make some modifications to the code in some of these modules, and want to reload it. Obviously, only one module can be interpreted at a time. So which one do we do first? It begs the question. What the `reload` command does is iterates over each module inside the package, reloading one after the other.

For now let's assume we're reloading `__init__.py` first.

Say inside `__init__.py`, we have this import line:
```py
from .module1 import *
```
So from here, python will go and look for whatever needs to be imported from `module1.py`. However, it's doing this *before we have a chance to reload `module1.py`*, hence whatever is imported will be from the *old module*.

The same thing will happen when we reload `module1.py`, if it depends in any way on `module2.py`.

Reloading a module after a separate module has already imported an object from it obviously will not change the behaviour of that imported object.

### In summary
This issue is dependent on the import order of the modules within your package. Sometimes, reloading will show the changes, sometimes it won't.

To solve this issue, we need to recursively reload modules which are *imported* from within other modules. However this recursion isn't particularly reliable if two modules import each other. You've struck gold on this one, RJM :)

Some pros of that would be

- Easier installation. We could do _everything_ through pip, and not require z3 to be externally installed and available in $PATH
- Performance. I expect there would be a nearly negligible performance improvement, as we would be making direct library calls, without the overhead of a pipe. Considering all the various optimizations we can implement for manticore, this would certainly be a micro-optimization
- Possibly simpler code in some areas. For example, we have some code for parsing z3 cli output for stuff like the version in solver.py that might be able to be removed.

Of these, I do think easier installation is a significant benefit for the project — the fact that we’ve required external z3 installation has always been a wart imo

I think that's all we'll do here - we don't want to move the sources, and I'd rather not rebuild a special release of the package with the deprecation in the readme: (a) it will take some time, and (b) it falsely indicates activity to have a new version published.

I just duplicated the error. I see:

> Module @jupyter-widgets/controls, semver range 1.0.0 is not registered as a widget module

This comes because the controls package was upgraded to be protocol version 1.1.0, but the python side still is looking for 1.0.0 (see https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/blob/b41d4dc189e6f5d21a20863384bfc879273326b6/packages/controls/src/version.ts#L9). There's a couple of ways we could solve this:

1. Register the controls package as every protocol version it supports (e.g., 1.0.0, 1.1.0, etc.)
2. Introduce some logic to do the automatic upgrade (i.e., instead of *just* looking for 1.0.0 controls, look for ^1.0.0 controls).

Expanding the initial test case.

Note: The pulp-client can be the server itself.

On a pulp-server

1. create repo1 and sync from upstream which has 2 versions of the same rpm

```
pulp-admin rpm repo create --repo-id repo1 --feed https://repos.fedorapeople.org/repos/pulp/pulp/demo_repos/test_multiple_rpm_versions/

pulp-admin rpm repo sync run --repo-id repo1

```

2. Find an RPM that has two different versions in the repo.

`pulp-admin rpm repo content rpm --repo-id repo1 --str-eq "filename=grinder_test_package-11.0-1.fc14.noarch.rpm"`

3. Create a new repo2, and copy the older of the rpm version into it.

```
pulp-admin rpm repo create --repo-id repo2
 pulp-admin rpm repo copy rpm -f repo1 -t repo2  --str-eq "filename=grinder_test_package-11.0-1.fc14.noarch.rpm"

pulp-admin rpm repo publish run --repo-id repo2

```
4. setup that repo2 on a host, and install the packages.
  This can be done 2 ways easily
Option 1
    Setup pulp.repo  in /etc/yum.repos/ with the repo2 from above
    Yum install the package

Option2
1. Install pulp-consumer packages
2. Register consumer to the pulp-server
3. Bind repo2 to the client
4. Use pulp-admin to install the package on the client

5. copy a newer version into the repo2 and re-publish

```
 pulp-admin rpm repo copy rpm -f repo1 -t repo2  --str-eq "filename=grinder_test_package-12.0-1.fc14.noarch.rpm"
pulp-admin rpm repo publish run --repo-id repo2

```

@rdpoor - The reason you have an "A" suffix is because you have a DUAL or QUAD ftdi device.

An example is the FTDI 2232 chip - it has 2 channels
Channel 0 - has the A suffix, Channel 1 has the B suffix.

In contrast, the FTDI 232 chip is a single channel..

All FTDI chips include (or support) an EEPROM of some sort (some are on chip, some are optional, it varies depending upon what you purchase)  there is a BIT in the EEPROM for each channel that tells the FTDI driver what mode it should be.

The WINDOWS driver - reads this bit and decides:  Enumerate the channel as COM =TRUE, or FALSE - do not enumerate as COM port.

The LINUX drivers generally ignore this bit.

Here it is:

Note about automatic model manager patches
===================================
In 0.8, Django-money automatically patches every model managers with MoneyManager. This causes migration problems if two or more managers are used in the same model. 

As a side effect, other managers are also finally wrapped with `MoneyManager`. This effect leads Django migration to point to fields with other managers to `MoneyManager`, and raises ``ValueError`` (`MoneyManager` only exists as a return of money_manager, not a class-form. However migration procedure tries to find `MoneyManager` for other managers.)

From 0.9, Django-money only patches `objects` with `MoneyManager` by default (as documented). To patch other managers (e.g. custom managers), patch them by wrapping with `money_manager`.

```!python
from djmoney.models.managers import money_manager
class BankAccount(models.Model):
    balance = MoneyField(max_digits=10, decimal_places=2, default_currency='USD')
    accounts = money_manager(MyCustomManager())
```

Vagrantfile is sorta like a Dockerfile or a Makefile, but for the vagrant toolset.

vagrant is a tool that makes it easier to bootstrap fresh virtualbox (or aws or vmware) instances where those instances are in a known working state. it goes a step further than what dev_requirements can offer, in that it installs all the system level stuff (build-essentials, gcc, python-dev, virtualenv, etc) when you're a developing across many different projects (which can have diff dep requirements).

for instance, i develop on ubuntu14 (using vagrant) on osx. and rather than having to blow away a virtual machine and rebuild from scratch manually, vagrant automates a lot of that base level work for me (running apt-get, etc). also, as a project maintainer if someone comes to me with a weird bug, we can both use a base state vm (leveraging vagrant) to help highlight where the bug might be, other than "hey, it works in my environment".

it doesn't seem like a lot, but i tend to work on more than 10-15 projects at a time [as i'm sure others do too], and the simple automation of quickly bringing up pre-configured VM's ready to go for a project drastically reduces the amount of overhead getting into a project (i understand it can be derived from the readme/dev_requirements, but automation of a build env can make all the difference towards a project imo)

see: https://www.vagrantup.com/docs/why-vagrant/

does that make sense? just trying to find ways to help others develop more pyzmq :)

Not sure if you're still having this issue. The event listener is actually running a simply http server that the sonos is sending notification to. Running two with cause the port collision you're seeing. I'd highly recommend having one listener that dispatches the notification to the appropriate handler.

If you must have two running you can alter [soco.config.EVENT_LISTENER_PORT](http://docs.python-soco.com/en/latest/api/soco.events.html?highlight=event_listener_port). By default it's set to 1400.

```
import soco
soco.config.EVENT_LISTENER_PORT=8080
````

Hi Dominik,

If you have an on-disk ctable called `mytable.bcolz`, you would open it by passing the `rootdir` parameter to the `ctable` constructor: `ct = bcolz.ctable(rootdir='mytable.bcolz')`. The `ct` variable is a `ctable` object that contains the metadata for accessing the content of the on-disk ctable; by itself, opening the on-disk table will not read the entire table into memory.

If you want to create an in-memory dataframe that contains entire columns from `ct`, you can use column-indexing (ala Pandas, `ct[['col1', 'col2']]`) to create another `ctable` object and call `.todataframe()` on that. For example, if `ct` has columns `A`, `B`, `C`, and `D`, but you only want columns `B` and `D`:

```
ct = bcolz.ctable(rootdir='mytable.bcolz')
df = ct[['B', 'D']].todataframe()
```

Hope that helps!

In general, this is great: I think multi-device support will be a great feature for DyNet to have. First, I have a high level comment. In my mind, there are two design decisions here:

### How do we specify the "default" device of a graph node when it is not specified explicitly
1. *Current Implementation:* A default is passed to ComputationGraph, and the default is used.
2. Alternatively, we could have the node default to the device of its first argument.

The first has the advantage of perhaps being easier to understand, but may result in hidden memory moves where people aren't expecting them. It also adds some code complexity.

### When some of the inputs are not on the same device what do you do?
1. *Current Implementation:* The ExecutionEngine is responsible for moving memory.
2. The ExecutionEngine throws an error, telling the user to move the memory themselves (using something like `dy.change_device(x, device)`).
3. A combination of 1. and 2., where 2. is on by default, but 1. can be chosen.

1. and 3. have the advantage of not crashing, but also have the potential to hide memory moves that the user really wouldn't want to be doing. (For example, in the example code, the weight matrix would be passed from CPU to GPU every time it was used, which would be really really bad.) 2. has the advantage of preventing this, but may result in a sightly increased coding burden.

*My opinion:* I tend to prefer 2./2. respectively, but could be convinced otherwise.

Reviving this thread. After checking existing implementations, it seems that people do different stuff and do support `adj` fairly often instead of `output_shape`:
Caffe2: adj only https://github.com/caffe2/caffe2/blob/master/caffe2/operators/conv_transpose_unpool_op_base.h#L45

MXNet: both

TensorFlow: output_shape

I'm a bit biased towards `adj` because it makes things work even with variable input size (though probably nobody is going to invoke it like that).

1) replace `output_shape` with `adj`

Hi Davide @davidedelerma 

I think differences you are observing stem from the resampling ingestion process performs. Even though you are ingesting in to the same CRS at the same scale there is still resampling that needs to be applied. Ingestor needs to align pixel boundaries, so it will perform sub-pixel translation even if you match crs and scale exactly, for that reason I would recommend against using `nearest neighbour` for ingestion as this would introduce artefacts when performing sub-pixel translation.

Try this:
ingestion-time: use cubic interpolation
load time for scene: use cubic interpolation
load time for ingested data: use nearest neighbour

With this you should get data that is much more similar or ideally the same.

Also ingestion process is not a requirement, if you are not performing re-projection or scale change you can just as well query your original data without incurring significant processing penalty, no need to ingest.

Basically the HTTP client holds a reference to the last response ([here](https://github.com/stripe/stripe-ruby/blob/d41a59fc82cd9989b16ebad9a4bcd9e39395466e/lib/stripe/stripe_client.rb#L167)), and the `request` method returns the reference in addition to the API resource ([here](https://github.com/stripe/stripe-ruby/blob/d41a59fc82cd9989b16ebad9a4bcd9e39395466e/lib/stripe/stripe_client.rb#L106)).

In our case we would do something similar, except that we'd copy the last response object onto the resource's `response` attribute.

> Ok, we might go we only __repr__ then?!

By my reading, if `__repr__` is defined and `__str__` is not, then Python will fall back to `__repr__`, so that should be fine.

@brandur-stripe My reasoning was that if we wanted to do _exactly_ the same thing that we did in stripe-ruby and stripe-php, we'd have to check if `subscription_item` was a key in ` params` and then raise an exception.

For the user though, it makes little difference. In nearly all cases, they'll be passing keyword arguments, e.g. calling `resource = stripe.UsageRecord.create(subscription_item='si_...', ...)`. If the `subscription_item` argument is missing, it makes little practical difference if the error that is returned is a `TypeError` returned by the interpreter because of the missing required argument, or an exception that we'd raise ourselves (because of the missing "required" argument).

I guess doing it this way does make it possible to pass `subscription_item` as a positional argument (e.g. `stripe.UsageRecord.create('si_...', ...)` but that's certainly not the intended usage. Python has a syntax for requiring a keyword argument ([PEP-3102](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-3102/)) but it's not available in Python 2 unfortunately.

I'm on the fence about all this, so if you think it's better, I can certainly switch to the manual exception raising pattern we've used in other libraries.

@aprasanth90, @holli-holzer The basic idea is this:

* get a template from somewhere
* render it into final XML using Django Templating or Mako or whatever
* inject that XML into the ._blob field of the target part, likely the DocumentPart object.

The template part can come from anywhere, but the last time I did it I stored it directly in the starting document as its document.xml part. I might like to do that differently in future because it limits what templating features you can use, is not well suited to version control if kept in its .docx (Zip) format, and is prone to corruption if edited in Word.

The template itself is based on the XML from one of the document 'parts'. The main document part is 'word/document.xml' when you unzip the .docx file. The opc-diag package is a handy way to get that neatly unzipped and pretty-printed ready for templating.

One aspect of this overall approach is you end up having to understand the WordprocessingML well enough to accomplish what you want. Usually that's easy enough by creating a document with Word that looks like what you want and then just finding the bits you want to change and replacing them with templating placeholders. But it does generally place it outside what's possible for end-users to do for themselves.

The rendering part I expect you understand well enough.

Once you have the final XML, you inject it into the DocumentPart something like this. Note the XML must be bytes before you inject it, not unicode. So depending on what you get back from your templating engine you may have to encode it as 'utf-8', e.g. `xml_bytes = xml_unicode.encode('utf-8')`:

```python
document = Document()
document_part = document._document_part
document_part._blob = xml_bytes_rendered_from_template
```

If you store the template in the starting document, you access it something like this:

```python
document = Document('my-template.docx')
document_part = document._document_part
template = document_part._blob
```

Note that python-docx supports opening a document from an expanded .docx zip archive. If your template .docx was expanded into the directory 'my-template', loading it would look like this:

```python
document = Document('my-template')
document_part = document._document_part
template = document_part._blob
```

I like that approach better than using a .docx file because you can keep the entire expanded template directory tree under version control with the rest of your source. This is what I referred to above as a "curated" template.

That should give you a start :)

Ok, a couple things.

1. in Word, the terms "header" and "heading" are both meaningful. A header runs across the top of the page within the top margin area, usually having document title and page number, that sort of thing. A heading (or more precisely a "section heading") is the bold (or whatever) short–ish text title that introduces a new section.

   I think what you're talking about here is headings, so lets keep the terminology straight. My prior response was based on the idea you needed something on headers, because that was what you asked for.

2. It matters whether all headings will be in Hebrew (RTL) or whether they're mixed RTL and LTR. So let me know and we'll go from there.

3. You'll need to read and understand this section in the docs (on styles) and the one that follows it (by hitting the "Next" button) before we proceed. My earlier suggestions might suddenly make more sense after :)  
http://python-docx.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user/styles-understanding.html

Hey @georgeyk, I've been thinking about this. Let me expose my points here to you, maybe you can help me:

- I'm not 100% ok with your approach because it hides from the client user about whether of not the token refresh is happening. In fact the user does not even know whether the wrapper supports or not token refreshing.
- Requiring a explicitly passed ``refresh_token=True`` parameter and raising a ``NotImplementedError`` is a good thing because it tells the client user that token refreshing is not currently supported and he might what to work on it and PR back to the repository.
- I understand that in most cases token refreshing is desirable to be an automatic operation. This is good because this way more inexperienced developers do not have to understand the mechanics and deal with it.
- At the same time, there might be a case where the client user does not want to automatically refresh the token and since Tapioca is a very generic solution, it must support this case as well.
- So far one solution that looks viable to me is:  we introduce a new parameter that can be passed on the initialization of the wrapper, maybe call it ``refresh_token_by_default``, that is ``False`` by default. But when the user sets it to ``True``, it will try to refresh token by default, unless ``refresh_token=False`` is passed in the request parameter.
- Since we are talking about this, there are other issues I'd like to tackle. For example, I was talking with @cassiobotaro this week, and we need a way to allow the user to retrieve the "updated token" so it can be saved for future use (otherwise the expired token will keep on).

This is what I have so far, what do you think? Any other ideas?

@piiswrong 

Here is the rationale behind the restructure:

First of all dividing the MXNet tutorials page into 

1. Gluon
2. MXNet

is like dividing a page on automobiles into 

1. Cars 
2. Automobiles. 

It doesn't make sense. That is what prompted the restructure. 

Following the reasons why it is restructured the way it is restructured now:

1. NDArray is common to both the imperative and symbolic interface. It is also the most basic tutorials. So, that goes first. 

2. After learning to use NDArray, user can use Autograd to automatically compute gradients. Autograd is independent from Gluon and can be used without Gluon. So, Autograd is the next tutorial. 

3. Between Gluon and the symbolic interface, Gluon is the easier and more intuitive interface. So, gluon tutorials comes next.

4. Symbolic interface naturally follows since Imperative (Gluon) and Symbolic are the two ways to write MXNet code. 

5. Hybrid blocks comes next because user needs to understand gluon and symbolic interface before user can understand Hybrid blocks. 

6. Sparse operations, large scale classifications and GAN are advanced topics and hence placed at the end.

Please let me know what if any you would like to change and why.

I could also do the same for queued_tasks which would reinstate the whole state of the executor after a restart, effectively closing the gap that we have between executor and scheduler (tasks are set to QUEUED when moved to the executor, but there is a delay (ie. heartbeat) before it is send to the MQ thus a kill leaves a window in which a QUEUED task can get lost).

That actually doesn't stop backfill. As soon as you un pause the dag, it backfills. 
This doesn't stop the backfill by default, only when set either in config, or on a per dag basis. Bolke and I came up with the design, and Paul said that Airbnb needs it as well. We also have several people asking on gitter for this.
In addition, this allows your own request for cron like behavior to work. This the allows us at apigee to move to the scheduler, from externally triggered dags (we use cron for all of our triggering now).
